3.5 The Solid State>Heat of fusion: heat to convert solid to liquid

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Why is condensation an exothermic process?

options.A: Gas particles gain kinetic energy when forming a liquid
options.B: Heat is absorbed from the surroundings to slow down gas particles
options.C: Gas particles release energy when they form intermolecular bonds in the liquid
options.D: The temperature of the gas must increase for condensation to occur

questions.hint: Think about bond formation vs. bond breaking.
questions.explanation: Condensation releases heat because gas particles lose kinetic energy and form intermolecular bonds, releasing energy to the surroundings. Evaporation is endothermic (bonds break).

explanations_tiered.definition: Condensation is exothermic because gas particles release energy as they form intermolecular bonds to become liquid.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: When gas condenses, particles slow down and attract each other, releasing the energy that was used to overcome forces during evaporation.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Steam turns to hot water on your skin — it releases heat because bonds form.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: During condensation, gas particles lose kinetic energy and form intermolecular attractions, releasing \(\Delta H_{\text{cond}} = -\Delta H_{\text{vap}}\) heat.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Condensation releases the enthalpy of vaporization \(\Delta H_{\text{vap}}\) as heat because the process is the reverse of the endothermic vaporization.
reasoning_steps: Recall that bond formation releases energy.
Condensation forms intermolecular bonds between particles.
misconceptions: Some think condensation absorbs heat because it cools surfaces; the surface cools because heat is transferred to the surroundings, not absorbed by the condensing gas.

glossary_entry.definition: Process that releases heat to the surroundings.

flashcard.back: Exothermic — heat is released when gas becomes liquid.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Gas particles release energy when forming intermolecular bonds in the liquid.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Condensation releases heat because gas particles form intermolecular bonds, giving off energy.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: What is the term for the direct change of a gas into a solid?
questions.hint: Think of frost forming from water vapor.
questions.explanation: Deposition is the direct phase change from gas to solid, skipping the liquid phase. Sublimation is solid to gas, condensation is gas to liquid, and freezing is liquid to solid.

source_trace.content_anchor: the change of vapor to solid

explanations_tiered.definition: Deposition is the phase transition where a gas changes directly into a solid without becoming a liquid.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Deposition is the reverse of sublimation and is exothermic; common examples include frost formation and snow formation from water vapor.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Frost on a cold window forms directly from water vapor in the air turning to ice.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Deposition occurs when gas particles lose enough kinetic energy to form a solid lattice directly, bypassing the liquid phase.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Deposition is a first-order phase transition where \(\Delta G = 0\), releasing \(\Delta H_{\text{dep}} = -\Delta H_{\text{sub}}\) heat.
reasoning_steps: Recall phase change from gas to solid.
misconceptions: Some confuse deposition with condensation (gas to liquid).

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Examples: frost, snow formation from vapor.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Deposition is the direct change of gas to solid.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: What is the molar heat of solidification (crystallization)?

options.A: Heat released when 1 mole of liquid freezes
options.B: Heat absorbed when 1 mole of solid melts
options.C: Heat released when 1 mole of gas condenses
options.D: Heat absorbed when 1 mole of solid sublimes

questions.hint: Think about the liquid-to-solid phase change.
questions.explanation: Molar heat of solidification is the heat released when one mole of liquid freezes to solid; it is equal in magnitude but opposite in sign to the heat of fusion. Option B describes heat of fusion (endothermic).

source_trace.content_anchor: The molar heat of crystallization (ΔH_cryst) is the quantity of energy that is removed from one mole of a liquid to convert it to the solid state at its freezing point

explanations_tiered.definition: Molar heat of solidification is the energy released when one mole of liquid freezes to solid at the freezing point.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: It is the opposite of fusion: while fusion absorbs heat (endothermic), solidification releases that same amount of heat (exothermic).
explanations_tiered.beginner: When water freezes to ice, it releases heat to the surroundings — that's the heat of solidification.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(\Delta H_{\text{cryst}} = -\Delta H_{\text{fus}}\); for water, \(\Delta H_{\text{cryst}} = -6.01\) kJ/mol.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Solidification is the reverse of fusion, so \(\Delta H_{\text{cryst}} = -\Delta H_{\text{fus}}\), and the process is exothermic.
reasoning_steps: Identify that this process releases heat.
misconceptions: Some think solidification absorbs heat; it releases heat (exothermic).

glossary_entry.definition: Heat released when 1 mol liquid freezes.

flashcard.front: What is molar heat of solidification?
flashcard.back: Heat released when 1 mole of liquid freezes.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Heat released when 1 mole of liquid freezes.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Molar heat of solidification is the heat released when 1 mole of liquid freezes.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which set of variables must remain constant when applying Avogadro's law to compare two gas samples?
questions.hint: Recall the conditions for Avogadro's law.
questions.explanation: Avogadro's law states that equal volumes of gases at the same temperature and pressure contain equal numbers of moles. Thus, temperature and pressure are constant. Option A describes Boyle's law conditions.

source_trace.content_anchor: explain the behavior of gases by using the variables volume, temperature, pressure and number of moles

explanations_tiered.definition: Avogadro's law applies at constant temperature and pressure: \(V \propto n\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: To compare volumes and moles using Avogadro's law, both temperature and pressure must be held constant so that the relationship \(V/n = \text{constant}\) holds.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Keep the temperature and pressure the same, then more gas takes more space.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Avogadro's law requires constant temperature and pressure; only then is volume directly proportional to moles.
explanations_tiered.advanced: From \(PV = nRT\), at constant \(T\) and \(P\), \(V = (RT/P)n\), so \(V \propto n\).
reasoning_steps: Recall the statement of Avogadro's law.
Identify the conditions: same T and P.
misconceptions: Some think volume is constant; Avogadro's law varies volume with moles at fixed T,P.

glossary_entry.definition: At constant T and P, equal volumes contain equal moles.

flashcard.front: What conditions are constant in Avogadro's law?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What stays constant in Avogadro's law?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Reinforce that Avogadro's law is derived from ideal gas law at fixed T,P.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Avogadro's law applies at constant temperature and pressure.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: What is the term for the temperature at which a liquid's vapor pressure equals exactly 1 atmosphere?
questions.hint: Think of standard conditions for boiling.
questions.explanation: The normal boiling point is defined as the temperature at which the vapor pressure of a liquid equals exactly 1 atm (760 torr). Melting point is solid-liquid equilibrium, critical temperature is above which gas cannot liquefy.

explanations_tiered.definition: Normal boiling point is the temperature at which a liquid's vapor pressure equals exactly 1 atmosphere (760 torr).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: At the normal boiling point, the liquid boils under standard atmospheric pressure; for water, the normal boiling point is 100°C.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Water boils at 100°C at sea level — that's its normal boiling point.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The normal boiling point is the boiling temperature when external pressure is exactly 1 atm; it's a standard reference point.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The normal boiling point is defined by \(P_{\text{vap}}(T_{\text{norm}}) = 1\) atm, and varies with intermolecular forces (higher ΔH_vap gives higher T_norm).
reasoning_steps: Recall that boiling point depends on external pressure.
Normal boiling point specifically uses 1 atm.
misconceptions: Some think boiling point is always 100°C; normal boiling point is at 1 atm, but boiling point changes with pressure.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Normal boiling point: T when P_vap = 1 atm.

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Distinguish from boiling point at other pressures.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. The normal boiling point is when vapor pressure equals exactly 1 atmosphere.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: A sample of gas has a volume of 5.0 L at 1.0 atm. If the pressure is increased to 2.5 atm at constant temperature, what is the new volume?
questions.explanation: \(V_2 = P_1V_1/P_2 = (1.0\,\mathrm{atm})(5.0\,\mathrm{L})/(2.5\,\mathrm{atm}) = 2.0\,\mathrm{L}\). Option C results from multiplying instead of dividing.

explanations_tiered.definition: Boyle's law: \(P_1V_1 = P_2V_2\) at constant temperature.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Pressure and volume are inversely proportional; increasing pressure decreases volume proportionally.
explanations_tiered.beginner: More pressure squeezes the gas into a smaller space.
misconceptions: Multiplying instead of dividing gives 12.5 L.

flashcard.front: Gas at 2.0 L and 3.0 atm is compressed to 1.0 atm at constant T. New volume?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: 5.0 L at 1.0 atm, P increased to 2.5 atm at constant T. Find V₂.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: P inversely proportional to V at constant T.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: How much heat is released when 36.0 g of water freezes at 0°C? (Molar heat of fusion of ice = 6.01 kJ/mol, molar mass of water = 18.0 g/mol)
questions.hint: Freezing releases heat equal in magnitude to heat of fusion.
questions.explanation: Moles = 36.0/18.0 = 2.00 mol. Heat released = 2.00 mol × 6.01 kJ/mol = 12.0 kJ (exothermic, so negative but magnitude 12.0 kJ). Option A is for 1 mole, option C is for 3 moles.

explanations_tiered.definition: Freezing releases heat equal in magnitude to the heat of fusion, but opposite in sign.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Calculate moles of water, then multiply by molar heat of fusion to find heat released during freezing.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Freezing water releases the same amount of heat as melting requires.
reasoning_steps: Report heat released as positive magnitude.
misconceptions: Using 36.0 g directly instead of converting to moles gives 36.0 × 6.01 = 216 kJ, not an option.

glossary_entry.definition: Heat released when liquid freezes, equal to ΔH_fus magnitude.

flashcard.front: Heat released when 54.0 g water freezes (ΔH_fus=6.01 kJ/mol, M=18)?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Heat released when 36.0 g water freezes? (ΔH_fus=6.01 kJ/mol)
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Remind that freezing is exothermic, so heat is released.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Moles = 36.0/18.0 = 2.00 mol, heat released = 2.00 × 6.01 = 12.0 kJ.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which statement correctly describes the behavior of real gases compared to ideal gases at high pressure?

options.A: Real gases occupy less volume than ideal gases because particles have volume
options.B: Real gases occupy more volume than ideal gases because particles have volume
options.C: Real gases behave exactly like ideal gases at all pressures
options.D: Real gases occupy less volume due to increased intermolecular attractions

questions.hint: Consider particle volume at high pressure.
questions.explanation: At high pressure, the finite volume of gas particles becomes significant, so real gases occupy more volume than ideal gases (which assume negligible particle volume). Option A is incorrect because attractions reduce volume, but volume exclusion dominates at high P.

explanations_tiered.definition: Real gases deviate from ideal behavior at high pressure due to finite particle volume and intermolecular forces.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: At high pressure, gas particles are forced closer together, so the volume they occupy (excluded volume) becomes significant, making real gases occupy more volume than ideal gas law predicts.
explanations_tiered.beginner: At high pressure, gas molecules take up space, so the gas is larger than the ideal law says.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Ideal gases assume negligible particle volume; real gas particles have finite volume, so at high P, the excluded volume increases the actual volume above PV=nRT predictions.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Van der Waals equation corrects for particle volume: \( (P + a(n/V)^2)(V - nb) = nRT \), where nb is excluded volume, increasing V at high P.
reasoning_steps: Recall ideal gas assumption: negligible particle volume.
At high pressure, particles are forced together.
Finite particle volume becomes significant → real gas occupies more volume.
misconceptions: Some think attractions dominate at high P; attractions reduce volume, but at very high P, volume exclusion dominates.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Real gases at high P: occupy more volume due to particle volume.

glossary_entry.definition: Gas with finite particle volume and intermolecular forces, deviating from ideal behavior.

flashcard.front: Why do real gases occupy more volume than ideal gases at high pressure?
flashcard.back: Finite particle volume (excluded volume) becomes significant.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How do real gases differ from ideal at high pressure?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: They occupy more volume due to finite particle size.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: High P: real gas volume > ideal due to excluded volume.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Discuss Van der Waals equation corrections.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. At high pressure, the finite volume of gas particles causes real gases to occupy more volume than ideal gases.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The combined gas law is expressed as \(\frac{P_1V_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2V_2}{T_2}\). If \(P_1 = 2.0\,\mathrm{atm}\), \(V_1 = 3.0\,\mathrm{L}\), \(T_1 = 300\,\mathrm{K}\), and \(T_2 = 600\,\mathrm{K}\) with \(P_2 = 1.0\,\mathrm{atm}\), what is \(V_2\)?
questions.explanation: \(V_2 = \frac{(2.0)(3.0)(600)}{(1.0)(300)} = \frac{3600}{300} = 12.0\) L. Option B results from omitting one pressure or temperature factor.

explanations_tiered.definition: The combined gas law relates pressure, volume, and temperature changes: \(\frac{P_1V_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2V_2}{T_2}\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Rearrange to solve for the unknown variable, then substitute given values to find the new volume.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Use the big formula that connects all three gas properties.
reasoning_steps: Use the combined gas law equation.
misconceptions: Forgetting to use kelvin or inverting ratios gives 3.0 L or 6.0 L.

flashcard.front: P₁=3 atm, V₁=4 L, T₁=300 K, T₂=600 K, P₂=2 atm. Find V₂.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Use combined gas law: P₁=2, V₁=3, T₁=300, T₂=600, P₂=1. Find V₂.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Ensure students use kelvin for temperature.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: How does the density of a gas compare to that of a liquid and a solid at the same temperature and pressure?

options.A: Gas density is highest, then liquid, then solid
options.B: Gas density is lowest, then liquid, then solid

questions.hint: Consider particle spacing in each state.
questions.explanation: Gas particles are far apart with much empty space, giving very low density. Liquids have closer particles, and solids the closest, so densities increase: gas < liquid < solid. Option A reverses the order.

explanations_tiered.definition: Density is mass per unit volume; gases have lowest density due to large empty space between particles.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Particle spacing: solids (tight), liquids (close), gases (far apart). Thus, gas density is lowest, liquid intermediate, solid highest.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Gas is light and spread out; solids are heavy and packed tight.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Because gas particles are very far apart with little mass per volume, gas density is much lower than liquid or solid density.
explanations_tiered.advanced: At STP, gas density ≈ 1-2 g/L, liquid density ≈ 1 g/mL (1000× higher), solid density slightly higher than liquid.
reasoning_steps: Recall particle spacing in each state.
misconceptions: Some think gases can be dense under high pressure; at same T and P, gas is always less dense than liquid or solid.

glossary_entry.definition: Mass per unit volume; gas density lowest.

flashcard.front: Order densities: gas, liquid, solid (lowest to highest).

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Gas density lowest due to particle spacing.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Note that water (ice) is an exception (ice floats).
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Gases have the lowest density, then liquids, then solids.
